Chevron entry to Guyana oilfields solves company's top challenge HOUSTON (Reuters) -Chevron’s imminent entry into Guyana’s rich offshore oilfields solves one of the biggest problems dogging the U.S. major: where its growth will come from beyond the next few years.  On Friday, the U.S. oil producer closed its $55-billion acquisition of Hess - among the largest ever oil and gas deals - and gained the latter's stake in Guyana's Stabroek Block after prevailing in a legal fight against larger rival Exxon Mobil.  Before the deal closed, concerns had been rising about Chevron's financial and production growth prospects, with its reserves of oil and gas dropping to the lowest in at least a decade.

Chevron closes Hess acquisition after winning Exxon legal battle STORY: Chevron sealed the deal for its $55 billion takeover of Hess on Friday, giving the energy giant access to the largest oil discovery in decades.Chevron completed the acquisition after winning a landmark legal battle against its larger rival Exxon Mobil, which had challenged the deal over rights to the Stabroek Block off the coast of Guyana.Exxon argued it had a pre-emptive right to buy Hess' stake in the lucrative offshore oil project, which holds more than 11 billion barrels of oil and is one of the fastest-growing sources of oil in the world.But the International Chamber of Commerce, the court that oversaw the case, ruled in favor of Chevron, and there is no appeals process.Stewart Glickman is an energy equity analyst at CFRA."Now that it's been decided, I think this is a shot in the arm for Chevron's, call it, medium-term production growth prospects because now it has acquired, among other things, the crown jewel, which is Guyana, 30% stake in this offshore oil project that has been throwing off a tremendous amount of production growth over the last couple of years and there are more phases to come that should also drive more incremental growth."Chevron CEO Mike Wirth's strategy to turn around his company's lagging performance hinged on the Hess acquisition, which had been delayed for over a year due to the high-stakes legal dispute.He said in a statement, quote, “This merger of two great American companies brings together the best in the industry.”Reuters previously reported that even as Chevron awaited the arbitration verdict, it was making preparations so it could close the deal with Hess quickly.Wirth said in an interview with Reuters on Friday that converting technology and combining employees from both companies would take a few months.Shares of Chevron were down about a percent in midday Friday trading while Exxon fell more than 3%.

Chevron's deal, first announced in October 2023, closed on Friday after a drawn-out dispute over Hess's 30% stake in Guyana's Stabroek block, the most attractive asset in its portfolio.  The offshore oilfield holds more than 11 billion barrels of oil and is one of the fastest-growing oil production regions in the world.

Chevron says zinc levels in US Mars oil output within limits HOUSTON (Reuters) -Zinc levels in flagship U.S. offshore crude Mars' production stream are now within expected levels after some contamination was identified in recent weeks, oil producer Chevron said on Wednesday, citing recent testing.  The start-up of an offshore well increased the levels of zinc in Mars crude earlier this month, leading to tightening crude supplies in the key Gulf Coast refining hub and prompting the U.S. government to release barrels from its emergency stockpile.  "We are not aware of any current compositional issues with Mars crude blend," Chevron said in an emailed statement.
